@@ -9,7 +9,7 @@
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0iyb","title":"[HIGH] ci: no concurrency: cancel-in-progress group — rapid PR pushes trigger duplicate CI runs (5-8x waste per PR)","description":"CI concurrency audit.

**Observation.** `.github/workflows/*.yml` — zero matches for `concurrency:` or `cancel-in-progress:`.

**What this means.** When an author pushes commit A to a PR branch, then pushes commit B 30 seconds later (fixup, rebase, etc.), GitHub Actions triggers TWO runs: one for A, one for B. Both fully run through all jobs (checks, fuzz-regression, etc.) even though only run B's result is relevant.

**Swarm-specific amplification.** Today's git log shows 20+ commits across 6 hours from multiple agents (cc-pandas + Dicklesworthstone + parallel workers + auto-rebase workflows). Every session with multiple rapid commits doubles, triples, or quadruples the CI load for no additional signal. br-0848138's `br flush` commit + my `pass-11` commit would trigger 2 runs; the `br flush` result doesn't matter because pass-11 is newer.

Rough math:
- Single PR with typical 5-8 commits: without concurrency group → 5-8 full CI runs (5-8 × ~7 min = 35-56 runner minutes).
- With concurrency group → only the final push's run completes (~7 min).
- Ratio: 5-8x waste.
- Swarm has ~20 commits/day × 7 min/run = 140 min/day CI cycles spent on superseded work.

**The standard fix.** Three lines at the top of each workflow:
```yaml
concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true
```

`group` identifies "same workflow, same branch/PR"; `cancel-in-progress: true` cancels the still-running older run when a new push lands. Exception on the main branch: use `cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}` so pushes to main never cancel each other (main branch runs are archival).

**Severity.** HIGH — compound cost across every PR in perpetuity. At this swarm's velocity, easily the cheapest bead in the backlog to pay off.

**Fix shape:**

1. Add `concurrency:` block to `.github/workflows/ci.yml` at workflow level:
   ```yaml
   name: CI
   on: [push, pull_request]

   concurrency:
     group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
     cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

   jobs:
     checks:
       ...
   ```

2. If any future workflow is added (br-4clx release workflow, br-36qc security workflow, br-ing6 nightly perf), each needs its own `concurrency:` group — or share the `group:` so they serialize / cancel together.

3. Consider a tighter group key for PR runs:
   ```yaml
   concurrency:
     group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
     cancel-in-progress: true
   ```
   The `pull_request.number || github.ref` idiom groups by PR number on PR events (always unique per PR) and by ref on direct pushes. Belt-and-suspenders version.

Blast radius: ci.yml + any future workflow file, 3-4 lines each. Zero source changes, zero test changes.

Prerequisites: None. Ship in isolation.

**Related:**
- br-ffhs (CI job splitting) — split jobs each need their own `concurrency:` group OR share one. Coordinate the two PRs.
- br-0l5r (cross-platform matrix) — matrix jobs should ALSO inherit the concurrency cancel so one matrix cell doesn't get canceled while another proceeds (generally fine with the above — whole workflow cancels).
- br-4clx (release workflow) — ships a new workflow, needs its own concurrency block.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:24:46.716318082Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-23T16:44:08.653341Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T16:44:08.653341Z","close_reason":"Already satisfied on main: ci.yml workflow-level concurrency block with PR-aware cancel-in-progress is in place since ffhs CI split","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","concurrency","review","waste"]}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0jmn","title":"pandas: Series.reset_index(name=...) value-column naming parity","description":"Python signature: pd.Series.reset_index(level=None, drop=False, name=..., inplace=False, allow_duplicates=<no_default>). Current fp-frame Series.reset_index only accepts drop: bool and cannot override the value column name on the DataFrame-return path. Implement the name override for single-index Series, preserve the existing drop=True Series branch, and add focused tests for named series, unnamed series, and collision handling with index/index-name columns.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:59:59.749325004Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:45:26.544815036Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:45:26.543000433Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0kb3","title":"fp-columnar Column.astype parity","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T06:59:50.285667679Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T07:10:15.788534132Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T07:10:15.786415942Z","close_reason":"Implemented Column::astype in commit 88836d2, 5 tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"frankenpandas-0kx7","title":"[MEDIUM] parity: GroupBy.apply result-shape semantics vs pandas 2.x","description":"Parity-gap audit (/reality-check-for-project).

**Scope.** pandas `DataFrame.groupby(keys).apply(func)` calls a user-provided function per group, returns a Series or DataFrame with the group keys as index. Pandas 2.x changed default `include_groups=True→False`; the result shape depends on what `func` returns.

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-frame has `DataFrame::groupby(...).apply(closure)` — surface check:

```
grep -n "pub fn apply\|GroupBy.*apply" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs  # find fn signatures
```

Gaps to verify:
- `.apply(func)` where `func` returns a DataFrame (pandas concatenates, preserving group key index).
- `.apply(func)` where `func` returns a Series of different length per group (pandas allows, produces sparse output).
- `.apply(func, include_groups=False)` — pandas 2.x default.
- Returning a scalar: pandas collapses to `Series` indexed by group keys.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — `.apply` is widely used for custom group logic; silent divergence from pandas result shape breaks user pipelines.

**Fix shape.**
1. Audit `DataFrameGroupBy::apply_*` signatures in fp-groupby.
2. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_groupby_apply.rs (10 inputs: empty groups, single-row groups, scalar-return, Series-return, DataFrame-return, mixed dtypes, ordered vs unordered groups, duplicate group labels, NaN group labels, `include_groups=False`).
3. DISCREPANCIES.md entry for any intentional shape divergence.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cod-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-25T02:11:25.394565113Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T03:22:56.613868144Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["apply","groupby","parity-gap","review"]}

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-frame has `DataFrame::groupby(...).apply(closure)` — surface check:

```
grep -n "pub fn apply\|GroupBy.*apply" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs  # find fn signatures
```

Gaps to verify:

- `.apply(func)` where `func` returns a Series of different length per group (pandas allows, produces sparse output).
- `.apply(func, include_groups=False)` — pandas 2.x default.
- Returning a scalar: pandas collapses to `Series` indexed by group keys.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — `.apply` is widely used for custom group logic; silent divergence from pandas result shape breaks user pipelines.

**Fix shape.**
1. Audit `DataFrameGroupBy::apply_*` signatures in fp-groupby.
2. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_groupby_apply.rs (10 inputs: empty groups, single-row groups, scalar-return, Series-return, DataFrame-return, mixed dtypes, ordered vs unordered groups, duplicate group labels, NaN group labels, `include_groups=False`).
3. DISCREPANCIES.md entry for any intentional shape divergence.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cod-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-25T02:11:25.394565113Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T03:40:25.832686128Z","closed_at":"2026-04-25T03:40:25.830716099Z","close_reason":"Completed in 9225b4d","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["apply","groupby","parity-gap","review"]}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0l5r","title":"[HIGH] ci: ubuntu-latest only — macOS/Windows cross-platform claim unverified","description":"Cross-platform CI audit.

**Observation.** .github/workflows/ci.yml line 10: `runs-on: ubuntu-latest`. No `strategy.matrix` defining a per-OS test matrix. No macOS runner. No Windows runner.

**Implications.**
- fp-io reads/writes Excel (.xlsx, .xls), CSV, Parquet, Feather, Arrow IPC. CSV line-ending semantics (LF vs CRLF) differ across OS. Excel / Parquet zip-archive handling via calamine / parquet-crate depends on OS-level file layer on some paths. None of this is tested on Windows, where the failure modes would appear.
- Path separators: fp-io has pub fn read_csv(path: &Path), pub fn read_parquet(path: &Path), etc. On Windows, a fixture path string produced on Linux with "/" may or may not resolve correctly depending on canonicalization. Not tested.
- Endianness: arrow / parquet byte manipulation is all little-endian; future big-endian fixtures or a PowerPC build would regress silently.
- The README advertises "Cross-platform (Linux/macOS/Windows)" under Installation implicitly by describing standard cargo build. Nothing validates that claim.

**Severity.** HIGH — product claim (cross-platform Rust library) is unverified. A Windows user of frankenpandas today is running untested code on every IO surface.

**Fix shape:**

ci.yml gains:
```
jobs:
  checks:
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    ...
```

Caveats and cost:
- Windows runners are ~2x slower than Ubuntu. Initial run will take ~12 min instead of 6.
- rch (remote-build helper) likely won't exist on Windows runners. Windows CI would build locally via `cargo test --workspace`.
- cargo-llvm-cov (installed but unused per sibling bead) works on macOS, limited on Windows; Windows job may need to skip the install.
- Some tests may rely on POSIX behavior: `std::fs::set_permissions` chmod bits, symlinks, etc. Expect 0-5 failures on the first Windows run that need per-OS cfg-gating or fixture adjustments. These become trackable beads.
- Excel path resolution on Windows uses \ separators; calamine's `open_workbook` may handle this but needs verification.

Incremental path if 3-OS matrix is too aggressive at once:
1. Start: ubuntu + macos (rch-compatible POSIX).
2. Next: add windows with only a `cargo check` + `cargo test -p fp-types -p fp-columnar -p fp-index` (no IO) to surface path-handling bugs without full IO breakage.
3. Finally: full `cargo test --workspace --all-targets` on windows.

Related: per-OS arrow/parquet/calamine versions don't diverge today (one workspace.dependencies block) — so any divergence in test behavior is a code bug, not a dep bug.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cc-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-22T19:59:30.233193403Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-23T16:23:19.122223754Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T16:23:19.119724019Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","cross-platform","review"]}
